texts.orig: 1. Introduction
texts.text: 1. Introduction

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Due to rapidly changing global climate in recent years, issues related to environmental and energy policies have become very important. In particular, there are on-going e ff orts for responding to global warming caused by the greenhouse e ff ect [1]. CO2, which is known to be the biggest cause of the greenhouse e ff ect, is produced from burning fossil fuels, such as petroleum, natural gas, and coal. In order to prevent global warming and achieve CO2 reduction goals in accordance with the Kyoto Protocol, it is important to eliminate CO2 from gases emitted by fossil fuel- fi red power plants [2].
texts.text: Due to rapidly changing global climate in recent years, issues related to environmental and energy policies have become very important. In particular, there are on-going e ff orts for responding to global warming caused by the greenhouse e ff ect [1]. CO2, which is known to be the biggest cause of the greenhouse e ff ect, is produced from burning fossil fuels, such as petroleum, natural gas, and coal. In order to prevent global warming and achieve CO2 reduction goals in accordance with the Kyoto Protocol, it is important to eliminate CO2 from gases emitted by fossil fuel- fi red power plants [2].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Techniques for capturing CO2 from gases emitted by power plants included absorption, adsorption, cryogenic, and membrane processes. The CO2 capturing method that is closest to becoming commercialized is the absorption column process using amine solution [3]. However, conventional packed column process for separating CO2 has various limitations, such as operational issues regarding fl ooding, foaming, entraining, and channeling, as well as high installation and operational cost [4]. Meanwhile, membrane contactor has received much attention as a promising technology that can overcome the disadvantages of packed column absorption process and replace that process [5].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In this study, commercial alpha alumina powder was used to fabricate hollow fi ber membrane by phase inversion spinning method. The fabricated alumina hollow fi ber membrane was con fi rmed to have high chemical and thermal stability based on thermogravimetric analysis and contact angle measurement. When CO2 stripping characteristics were examined by introducing decompression membrane contactor process in the stripping part of amine absorption process, increase in degree of vacuum resulted in increased CO2 stripping fl ux. In liquid to lumen operation under decompression pressure of 61.325 kPa and absorbent fl ow rate of 9 ml/min, CO2 stripping fl ux was 45.75 × 10 -4 mol/m 2 ·s. Existing articles on membrane contactor stripping were mostly N2 sweep type and reported CO2 stripping fl ux of 1 -24 mol/m 2 ·s, whereas the present study achieved stripping performance that was approximately 2 -40 times higher. Decompression stripping of porous ceramic hollow fi ber membrane contactor can be viewed as a promising process since it did not cause the phenomenon of decrease in CO2 purity due to N2 and showed similar or greater CO2 stripping fl ux than previously reported membrane contactor stripping processes.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Membrane contactors can independently control the fl ow of gas and liquid. Therefore, stable process operation is possible at fl ow rate low enough not to allow in fl ux of liquid into the packed column or at fl ow rate that exceed the speed limit due to fl ooding [6]. Moreover, because the membrane plays the role of providing a large gas-liquid interface, it has a much larger contact surface than packed column process. Based on these advantages, equipment needed for CO2 capturing process can be greatly reduced [7].

texts.orig: Amine absorption process can be divided largely into two parts; absorption and stripping. Previously reported studies on membrane contactors were conducted mostly for replacing the absorption part. In particular, studies have been actively conducted on membrane contactors using hydrophobic materials as the material for porous polymeric membrane [8 -11]. Although the development of polymeric membrane contactors with excellent performance in CO2 separation process has taken place, there have been reports of rapid decline in performance due to wetting during continuous operation [12]. Recently, attention has been focused on the potential of applying membranes fabricated with ceramic materials, which fundamentally has excellent chemical and thermal stability, in CO2 capturing process. Our research team has previously reported on membrane contactor CO2 absorption process using hydrophobic ceramic hollow fi ber membrane contactor [7,13 -15].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: On the other hand, studies on application of membranes for stripping, which accounts for most of energy consumption in wet amine absorption process, are very much lacking [16]. A study by S. Khaisri et al. [12] analyzed the CO2 stripping performance in monoethanolamine (MEA) using PTFE hollow fi ber membrane contactor. However, such polymeric membranes have low thermal stability and it has been reported that decline in performance and damage to the membrane can occur from pore swelling caused by basic absorbent. In particular, CO2 stripping process takes place at high temperatures, which presents limitations in applying polymeric membranes. Recently, some researches on polymeric membrane contactors having improved chemical and thermal stability has been reported. GTI in the United States has demonstrated a poly(ether ether ketone) (PEEK) hollow fi ber membrane contactor process. PEEK hollow fi ber membranes have been reported to have high stability in absorption -desorption processes [17]. Glassy polymers such as poly(1-trimethylsylil)-1-propyne) (PTMSP) are well known to exhibit low chemical stability against aliphatic and aromatic hydrocarbons [18]. However, Volkov et al. recently announced that glassy polymers are stable with amine absorbents at high temperature [19]. Another way to overcome such disadvantages of traditional polymeric membranes, the research team led by Professor Li presented a process of CO2 stripping in MEA using ceramic hollow fi ber membrane contactor [16]. However, most of the reported studies on membrane contactor stripping used sweep gas, such as N2, for enhancing stripping performance. However, when a sweep gas is used, N2 and CO2 separated in the absorption part become mixed again to cause appreciable decline in separation performance of the overall process. Therefore, there is a very low possibility of applying membrane contactor stripping process that uses sweep gas in actual CO2 capturing process.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Recently, there are studies being conducted on the development of membrane contactor stripping process for increasing the purity and recovery of separated CO2 without using sweep gas. The research team led by Professor Kim played a central role in developing a membrane contactor stripping process based on low-temperature vacuum stripping [20]. They used membrane with porous polyethylene (PE) sca ff old coated with dense, thin- fi lm silicalite- fi lled polydimetylsiloxane (PDMS). CO2 stripping performance was enhanced by introducing a dense membrane coating layer and using high vacuum instead of N2 sweep. However, due to the chemical and thermal limitations of polymeric membrane, decline in performance according to operating time was reported. Moreover, because much energy is consumed for high vacuum decompression, it is di ffi cult to expect improvement in overall energy e ffi ciency.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: When vacuum decompression is applied to porous membrane contactor in CO2 stripping process, the absorbent can move through the pores of the membrane. Because of this reason, studies on decompression using porous membrane contactors have not taken place. However, such phenomenon can be resolve by operating at low vacuum range and making the membrane hydrophobic enough. Because low vacuum is used, improved process energy e ffi ciency could be expected as well. Moreover, using ceramic hollow fi ber membrane with high thermal and chemical stability is also forecasted to show improved process stability. The objective of the present study was to present a novel stripping technique of low vacuum decompression stripping technique using porous ceramic membrane for reduction of stripping energy, which accounts for approximately 80% of the total operating cost among amine absorption technique, a technique for CO2 recovery after combustion using aqueous amine solution. The ceramic hollow fi ber membrane fabricated by phase inversion was hydrophobically modi fi ed to construct the membrane contactor module. During vacuum stripping, the vacuum-side pressure was varied between approximately 61.325 and 101.325 kPa (abs.) to examine the stripping characteristics according to degree of vacuum.

texts.orig: 2.1. Fabrication of hydrophobic ceramic hollow fiber membrane
texts.text: 2.1. Fabrication of hydrophobic ceramic hollow fiber membrane

texts.label: text
texts.orig: For alumina (Al2O3) used for fabrication of hollow fi ber membrane, commercial powder with average particle size of 0.5 μ m and purity of 99.9% was purchased from Kceracell. To prepare the dope solution for hollow fi ber membrane spinning, polyethersulfone (PESf; Ultrason ® E6020P, BASF, Germany) was dissolved the solvent, 1-Methyl-2-pyrrolidone (NMP; 99.5%, Sanchun Pure Chemical CO., Ltd, Korea), by stirring at room temperature for approximately 12 h. Alumina powder and polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP; Sigma Aldrich, USA) was added to this solution and mixed for approximately 24 h to prepare the dope solution. The dope solution tank was evacuated with a vacuum pump (IDP3, VARIAN, U.S.A.) for approximately 1 h to degas the bubbles that had formed during the stirring process.
texts.text: For alumina (Al2O3) used for fabrication of hollow fi ber membrane, commercial powder with average particle size of 0.5 μ m and purity of 99.9% was purchased from Kceracell. To prepare the dope solution for hollow fi ber membrane spinning, polyethersulfone (PESf; Ultrason ® E6020P, BASF, Germany) was dissolved the solvent, 1-Methyl-2-pyrrolidone (NMP; 99.5%, Sanchun Pure Chemical CO., Ltd, Korea), by stirring at room temperature for approximately 12 h. Alumina powder and polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP; Sigma Aldrich, USA) was added to this solution and mixed for approximately 24 h to prepare the dope solution. The dope solution tank was evacuated with a vacuum pump (IDP3, VARIAN, U.S.A.) for approximately 1 h to degas the bubbles that had formed during the stirring process.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The hollow fi ber membrane was fabricated by phase inversion spinning method. The spinning pressure of the hollow fi ber membrane dope solution was adjusted to 1 bar by feeding nitrogen. At the same time, a pulseless syringe pump (Fusion 100, Chemyx, U.S.A.) was used to feed the internal coagulant at a fl ow rate of 10 ml/min. The air gap, which is the distance between the tip of the nozzle and the surface of the coagulation bath, was maintained at 10 cm and the spun hollow fi ber membrane was placed in the coagulation bath for approximately 24 h to allow formation of a porous structure by phase inversion. Here, the internal and external coagulant used was distill water and tap water, respectively. After the phase inversion process, the hollow fi ber membrane was washed 3 times with clean tap water to remove any residual solvent. After washing, the hollow fi ber membrane was dried in an oven (120 °C) for approximately 24 h, after which, it was sintered in a box furnace (1300 °C) for 4 h. The sintered hollow fi ber membrane was hydrophobically modi fi ed using heptadeca fl uoro-1,1,2,2-tetrahydrodecyltrimethoxysilane (FAS, ≥ 98%, Sooyang Chemtec Co., Ltd, Korea). OH-group exists on the surface of an alumina hollow fi ber membrane, it has hydrophilic property. To modify the hydrophobic surface, FAS - that are bindable with OH-groups and have hydrophobic chains on the opposite side- was used.

texts.orig: To check the pore structure of the fabricated ceramic hollow fi ber membrane, cross section and surface of the membrane were analyzed using a fi eld emission scanning electron microscope (FE-SEM; S-4800, Hitachi, Japan). Moreover, thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) was performed to check the thermal stability of the hydrophobic modi fi ed ceramic hollow fi ber membrane. Change in mass of the hollow fi ber membrane was observed using a thermogravimetric analyzer (SDT Q600, TA Instruments, USA) while altering the temperature from room temperature to 900 °C at a heating rate of 10 °C/min under N2 atmosphere. texts.label: text
texts.orig: In the fi rst step of modularization, the hollow fi ber membranes were cut to a certain length to produce a membrane contactor module having a constant membrane area. The cut hollow fi ber membranes were sealed into bundles with 5 strands each using epoxy adhesive and inserted into the housing. To con fi rm that the fabricated module was completely sealed with no leaks, inside of the module was evacuated and check to make sure the state of vacuum was maintained for a set period. Moreover, to check whether the membrane had cracked during the potting process or that the sealant (epoxy adhesive) was injected properly for sealing the membrane, distilled water was injected into the shell side of the module to check that water did not leak into the lumen side of the hollow fi ber membrane. Module with con fi rmed sealing by such 2-step leak testing was used to carry out the CO2 stripping experiment.

The cut hollow fi ber membranes were sealed into bundles with 5 strands each using epoxy adhesive and inserted into the housing. To con fi rm that the fabricated module was completely sealed with no leaks, inside of the module was evacuated and check to make sure the state of vacuum was maintained for a set period. Moreover, to check whether the membrane had cracked during the potting process or that the sealant (epoxy adhesive) was injected properly for sealing the membrane, distilled water was injected into the shell side of the module to check that water did not leak into the lumen side of the hollow fi ber membrane. Module with con fi rmed sealing by such 2-step leak testing was used to carry out the CO2 stripping experiment.

In the decompression side of the membrane, the degree of vacuum was controlled using a vacuum pump. Moreover, gas from the vacuum pump passed through a moisture trap and was analyzed by gas chromatography (GC).

texts.orig: Table 1 shows the speci fi cations of the ceramic hollow fi ber membrane module that was fabricated. Pore size and e ff ective surface porosity were calculated by Eqs. (2) -(4) using the N2 gas permeance result. Fig. 2 shows the SEM images of the cross section (a) , lumen side (c) , and shell side (d) of the hydrophobic modi fi ed ceramic hollow fi ber membrane. The images con fi rmed that the ceramic hollow fi ber membrane is porous and the pores were not blocked due to the hydrophobic coating material (FAS) forming a thick layer above the membrane. Based on gas permeance, it was determined that the pores identi fi ed by the SEM images were open pores. Moreover, based on the EDS results on fl uorine atoms shown in Fig. 2 (b) , it was con fi rmed that the hydrophobic FAS coating layer was uniformly coated even into the pores of the hollow fi ber membrane. Hydrophobicity by the FAS coating layer was con fi rmed by measuring contact angle and breakthrough pressure. The contact angle before the coating was 90° or less, but the contact angle after coating was about 120° or more. The breakthrough pressure was 1.05 ± 0.02 bar before coating and 3.04 ± 0.04 bar after coating process.

texts.orig: Fig. 3 shows the results for single gas permeance of the hydrophobic modi fi ed ceramic hollow fi ber membrane. The volume of gas permeating through the membrane pores increased with increase in pressure. Although the permeance of N2 was slightly higher than that of CO2, the selectivity of membrane itself was not meaningful for separating CO2. According to Eq. (1), fl ow by Knudsen di ff usion is inversely proportional to 0.5th power of the molecular weight. Because the molecular weight is N2 smaller relative to CO2, the results shown in Fig. 3 are consistent with the mechanism of Knudsen di ff usion. It is known that Knudsen di ff usion is dominant when the pore size of the membrane is < 1 × 10 -7 m [22].

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4. Conclusions
texts.text: 4. Conclusions

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In the present study, a novel technique of low vacuum decompression stripping using porous ceramic membrane for stripping energy reduction was developed. The alumina hollow fi ber membrane was fabricated by phase inversion spinning using commercial powder, while FAS was used to prepare the hydrophobic modi fi ed stripping module. When the CO2 stripping characteristics were examined after introducing the decompression membrane contactor process in the stripping side of amine absorption process, CO2 stripping fl ux increased as the degree of vacuum increased. Moreover, liquid to shell operation showed higher stripping fl ux than liquid to lumen operation at low degree of vacuum. As the degree of vacuum became higher, liquid to lumen operation showed higher stripping fl ux than liquid to shell operation and eventually showed similar result at 61.325 kPa (abs). When the fl ow rate of the absorbent was 9 ml/min in liquid to lumen operation under decompression at 61.325 kPa, CO2 stripping fl ux was 45.75 × 10 -4 mol/m 2 ·s. In conclusion, it is believed that low vacuum decompression stripping of membrane contactor is a promising alternative that can reduce energy consumption and increase stripping effi ciency without decrease in CO2 purity due to N2.
texts.text: In the present study, a novel technique of low vacuum decompression stripping using porous ceramic membrane for stripping energy reduction was developed. The alumina hollow fi ber membrane was fabricated by phase inversion spinning using commercial powder, while FAS was used to prepare the hydrophobic modi fi ed stripping module. When the CO2 stripping characteristics were examined after introducing the decompression membrane contactor process in the stripping side of amine absorption process, CO2 stripping fl ux increased as the degree of vacuum increased. Moreover, liquid to shell operation showed higher stripping fl ux than liquid to lumen operation at low degree of vacuum. As the degree of vacuum became higher, liquid to lumen operation showed higher stripping fl ux than liquid to shell operation and eventually showed similar result at 61.325 kPa (abs). When the fl ow rate of the absorbent was 9 ml/min in liquid to lumen operation under decompression at 61.325 kPa, CO2 stripping fl ux was 45.75 × 10 -4 mol/m 2 ·s. In conclusion, it is believed that low vacuum decompression stripping of membrane contactor is a promising alternative that can reduce energy consumption and increase stripping effi ciency without decrease in CO2 purity due to N2.
